question: What is the OWASP Mobile Top 10 and what should every mobile dev know about it?
answer:
OWASP Mobile Top 10 (latest 2024 list) — the most common security risks in mobile apps. Highlights:

• M1 Improper Credential Usage — hardcoded API keys, secrets in source.
• M2 Inadequate Supply Chain Security — vulnerable third-party SDKs.
• M3 Insecure Authentication / Authorization — weak token storage, missing biometric checks.
• M4 Insufficient Input/Output Validation — XSS in WebViews, SQL/JSON injection.
• M5 Insecure Communication — missing TLS, weak ciphers, no certificate pinning.
• M6 Inadequate Privacy Controls — over-collection of PII, no consent.
• M7 Insufficient Binary Protections — no obfuscation/integrity checks where appropriate.
• M8 Security Misconfiguration — debug endpoints in prod, exposed `exported=true` components.
• M9 Insecure Data Storage — secrets in plain SharedPreferences / UserDefaults.
• M10 Insufficient Cryptography — broken algorithms, custom crypto, missing IVs.

What to do: thread these through code review, run static analysis (Mobile Security Framework, Detekt rules, AppSweep), and run penetration tests on critical apps.

question: How do you protect API traffic on mobile?
answer:
Multi-layer defenses:

• TLS — always. Disable cleartext traffic via `android:usesCleartextTraffic=false` + iOS ATS. Enforce TLS 1.2+; reject older.
• Certificate / SPKI pinning — defends against MITM with rogue CAs. Bundle one or more SPKI hashes; rotate ahead of expiry.
• HSTS / well-known TLS configs — server side too.
• OAuth 2.0 / OIDC with PKCE — never roll your own auth flow.
• Short-lived access tokens (~15 min) + refresh tokens. Refresh tokens stored in Keychain / Keystore.
• Device attestation — Play Integrity API (Android), App Attest / DeviceCheck (iOS) — server validates that requests come from a real, unmodified app.
• Replay protection — server-side nonce + request signing for sensitive endpoints.

Don't rely on obfuscation or certificate pinning alone — both can be bypassed by determined attackers. Layer with server-side checks.

question: How do you detect rooted / jailbroken devices and what should you do about it?
answer:
Detection signals:

Android (rooted):
• Presence of `/system/xbin/su`, `/system/bin/su`, busybox.
• Test-keys in build fingerprint.
• `getprop ro.debuggable = 1` in production.
• Magisk / Zygisk hide modules — harder; use Play Integrity device verdict.

iOS (jailbroken):
• Presence of `/Applications/Cydia.app`, `/private/var/lib/apt`.
• Ability to write outside the sandbox (`/private/`).
• `cydia://` URL scheme handlers.
• Unsandboxed file access tests.

Libraries: RootBeer (Android), DTTJailbreakDetection / IOSSecuritySuite (iOS).

What to do:
• Don't block all rooted/jailbroken users — many devs and power users have rooted their dev phones.
• Restrict sensitive actions: refuse to enable biometric login, deny payments, downgrade trust level.
• Combine with server-side device attestation (Play Integrity, App Attest) — these are harder to bypass than client-side checks.
• Log + monitor; never trust the client alone.

question: How do you store cryptographic keys and use the platform secure enclave?
answer:
Both platforms expose hardware-backed key storage:

• Android — Android Keystore (`AndroidKeyStore` provider). Generate keys with `KeyPairGenerator` / `KeyGenerator` for `EC`, `RSA`, `AES`. Set `setIsStrongBoxBacked(true)` for StrongBox-backed keys (where supported). Never extract key material; only sign / encrypt via the system.
• iOS — Secure Enclave (`SecAccessControl` + `kSecAttrTokenIDSecureEnclaveTrue`). EC keys (P-256) for sign/verify. CryptoKit (`SecureEnclave.P256.Signing.PrivateKey`) gives a Swifty API.

Both let you require user authentication (PIN / biometric) before operations.

Key rotations: server should accept multiple public keys per user during rotation; rotate keys on suspicious events (re-install, biometric change, jailbreak detected).

Don't roll your own crypto. Use AES-GCM (256-bit) for symmetric, ECDSA / Ed25519 for signatures, ECDH for key agreement. RSA only when interop demands it.

question: How do you handle sensitive data on the screen (screenshots, screen recording, clipboard)?
answer:
Defenses against on-device snooping:

Screenshots & screen recording:
• Android — set `WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE` on Activities containing secrets (banking, password reveal). Prevents screenshots, hides from screen-recordings, blurs in recents.
• iOS — listen for `UIScreen.capturedDidChangeNotification` (recording in progress). For screenshots, observe `UIApplication.userDidTakeScreenshotNotification`. To blur the app preview when sent to background, set a placeholder view in `applicationWillResignActive`.

Clipboard:
• Avoid auto-copying secrets. If you must, schedule a clear after a few seconds.
• Android 12+ shows a toast when an app reads the clipboard — assume any read is observable.
• iOS — set `expirationDate` on `UIPasteboard` items; mark `UTType.passkit` / generic `kUIPasteboardTypeListEncryptedString` for sensitive items where applicable.

Keyboard:
• Disable autofill suggestions / personalised learning on sensitive fields (`textNoSuggestions`, `textPassword` on Android; `textContentType = .oneTimeCode` etc. on iOS).
• Use the platform-provided secure input (passwords, payment) — they hide content from screen recording.

question: How do you handle privacy: tracking, ATT, GDPR, Privacy Manifests?
answer:
Multi-jurisdictional privacy is a product + engineering concern.

Apple (iOS):
• ATT (App Tracking Transparency) — show `ATTrackingManager.requestTrackingAuthorization` BEFORE any cross-app tracking SDK runs. Status determines if you can use IDFA.
• Privacy Nutrition Labels — declare data types collected + linked to user identity in App Store Connect. Auto-rejection if mismatched.
• Privacy Manifests (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` since iOS 17.4) — declare API usage reasons (NSPrivacyAccessedAPI*) for things like UserDefaults, system boot time. Required for new submissions.

Google (Android / Play):
• Data Safety section in Play Console — what you collect, share, retain, encrypt.
• Advertising ID (AAID) requires `com.google.android.gms.permission.AD_ID` permission since Android 13. Users can reset / delete it.
• Health Connect, location, photo picker — narrower permission scopes encouraged.

GDPR / global:
• Lawful basis for processing — consent / legitimate interest / contract.
• User rights — access, deletion, portability. Build endpoints to honor them.
• Cookie / tracker consent before loading non-essential SDKs.
• Data residency — for EU/UK, often pin storage to that region.

Practices:
• Inventory every SDK and document data flows.
• Delay non-essential SDK init until after consent is given.
• Encrypt PII at rest. Hash + salt identifiers where possible.
• Audit annually + on every new vendor.
